_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for King William County, VA. Record survival for the county between the Pamunkey and Mattaponi Rivers — records survive from 1702 (county formation) with moderate completeness.
display_name: King William County, Virginia — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: King William, VA — Route 30 at the courthouse village

previous_courthouses.location: King William Court House village — on the road between the Pamunkey and Mattaponi Rivers
previous_courthouses.fate: The antebellum courthouse was destroyed by fire in 1885. This was the primary record loss event for the county.

previous_courthouses.location: King William Court House — rebuilt after the fire

record_losses.description: The King William County courthouse burned in 1885, destroying many records. Unlike the Civil War losses in neighboring counties, this was a peacetime fire that destroyed irreplaceable documents. However, the loss was not total — some bound volumes survived the fire, and the Library of Virginia holds copies and fragments. The extent of surviving records varies by record type.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1885 courthouse fire in King William County creates a genealogical wall for many families. If {ancestor_name}'s court appearance, estate settlement, or apprenticeship record was in a volume that burned, the fire ended the paper trail — but deed and will books that survived may still document land transfers and inheritance.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Partial — some deed books survive from county formation (1702) but others were destroyed in the 1885 fire. Coverage is uneven.
deeds.gaps: Significant gaps where individual volumes were destroyed. Post-1885 deeds are complete.
deeds.repository: King William County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Partial — some will books survive from county formation. Coverage is uneven due to the 1885 fire.
wills_estates.gaps: Some will books destroyed. Estate loose papers are largely lost for the pre-1885 period.
wills_estates.repository: King William County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds survive from approximately 1783. Some gaps due to the 1885 fire.
marriages.gaps: No bonds before 1783. The fire destroyed some later marriage records as well.
marriages.repository: King William County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Heavily damaged by the 1885 fire. Most order books before 1885 were destroyed.
court_minutes.gaps: Major gaps throughout the pre-1885 period. Court minutes are the record type most affected by the fire.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; King William County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events in church records.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

substitute_sources.source_type: King and Queen County records (parent county)
substitute_sources.description: King William was formed from King and Queen County in 1702. King and Queen's records — which also suffered a courthouse fire in 1864 — may document King William families before the 1702 division.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; King and Queen County Clerk of Court
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For families in King William County at its formation, the parent county's records (where they survive) document the pre-1702 period.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: St. John's Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) served King William County. Parish records provide an alternative documentation source when county records are lost.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; Virginia Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish vestry records include tithable lists, processioning records, and poor relief entries. These function as substitute civil records when courthouse records are destroyed.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Virginia parish record collections.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Critical for a county with significant record losses.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records provide the baseline for family reconstruction when county records are fragmentary. The Pamunkey and Mattaponi reservations were enumerated separately in some census years.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, King William County, VA.

substitute_sources.description: The Pamunkey and Mattaponi tribes maintained their own records — tribute lists, membership rolls, and governance documents — independent of the county court system.
substitute_sources.repository: Pamunkey Indian Museum; Mattaponi tribal archives; Smithsonian Institution
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For researchers tracing Pamunkey or Mattaponi ancestry, tribal records supplement and sometimes replace county records. The annual tribute lists document tribal membership across centuries.
substitute_sources.source: Rountree, Helen C. Pocahontas's People. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 1990.

narrative_hooks: The 1885 courthouse fire in King William County destroyed records that had documented families since 1702 — if {ancestor_name}'s court record was in the wrong volume that day, it is gone, and the search must turn to church records, census data, and neighboring county files.

King William's parent county — King and Queen — also lost records to fire (1864), creating a double wall for families who lived in the area before 1702. But between the surviving volumes from both counties, some chains can be reconstructed.

The Pamunkey and Mattaponi people maintained their own records independent of the county courthouse — and if {ancestor_name} had any connection to the reservations, the tribal records may document what the 1885 fire destroyed.

Census records become essential in King William County because the courthouse fire eliminated so much of the civil record — for {ancestor_name}, the federal enumerator's visit every ten years may be the most reliable surviving documentation.
source_references: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Rountree, Helen C. Pocahontas's People: The Powhatan Indians of Virginia Through Four Centuries. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 1990.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, King William County, VA.
